entries: Through the inept and unthinking process of evolution, parasitic fish still attempt to parasitise the vampiacrus. With the pulses of electricity that ripple across its dermis, these parasites are instantly fried and reanimated, becoming thralls of the enormous sea creature—children the vampiacrus entirely neglects. When these prized, glowing minnows are inevitably caught as bycatch, they enter a life of mutualism: miners use these fish as flameless light sources, keeping them in helmet-mounted fishbowls and reinvigorating them with bursts of electricity or drops of blood. The more superstitious underground labourers reject this bio-technological marvel, claiming the fishes' master can see through their eyes, and that if enough are brought together, a vampiacrus may appear!
{@i Was it worth shaving three years off my life for a cape made of smoke? Probably not, but I'm gonna look fantastic at the gala.}
entries.by: Edward Goose, winner of both "Best Dressed" and "Most Likely to Die Young"

entries.name: Lust for Immortality
entries: Like many others of their kind, thanatophobia jinn represent a mortal desire: the wish to live forever. They're willing to sacrifice anyone or anything in pursuit of that goal. For these jinn, the fear of death is more merited than most, as thanatophobia jinn have very little natural life force of their own, and they slowly fade without absorbing the vitality of others.

entries.name: Trading in Time
entries: Even with their reputation for stealing away life itself, many still seek out thanatophobia jinn for the deals they offer. The young, ambitious, and impatient readily sacrifice the sunset years of their lives for minor boons or collected oddities. Thanatophobia jinn thrive in college towns and tourist destinations, and cities with multiple thanatophobia jinn have demonstrably lower average lifespans.

entries.name: Bloodmist Form
entries: While thanatophobia jinn appear humanoid most of the time—usually favoring a draconic form, presumably due to dragons' longevity, but no one but the jinn themselves know for sure—their true nature isn't even corporeal. Thanatophobia jinn are made up of pure life force held together by emotional drive, and they can split off parts of themselves freely. These tiny scattered fragments, mimicking the wings of dragons or bats, can leech victims of their vitality, directing it back to the jinn.

entries.name: Psiophage

entries: {@i Wandering Thoughts has been freaking out all day! It keeps projecting horrible images of greyscale clowns. I don't think I want to meet whatever spooked it.}

Once thought to be some kind of madness-inducing illness or mental condition afflicting psionic creatures, psiophages are incorporeal, parasitic predators whose prey comprise the telepathic and empathic. A psiophage unravels every shred of personality and intelligence and replaces its victim's psyche with its own.

entries.name: Thought Parasite
entries: While gifted arcanists and hiveminds are their favourite prey, a psiophage can sink its vicious teeth into any creature with telepathic abilities (note: they don't actually have teeth). It can sniff out potential victims by following the trail of their telepathic waves;the stronger the psychic ability is, the more delicious the scent (note: they can't actually smell). After latching onto its host, a psiophage devours its mind, replacing all thoughts, personality, and emotions with its own, eventually twisting the physical form to its own warped interpretation of a person (note: this one isn't metaphorical, it actually does this).

entries.name: Mimeflayers
entries: {@i Parasitic Imitator}
{@b Habitat:} The Low; {@b Treasure:} None

Many denizens of The Low, with their natural prowess in the telepathic arts, make prime prey for a psiophage. The parasite's grasp of anatomy is already poor, and the additional tentacles of a mind flayer are no easier for it to comprehend. The psiophage twists the tentacled visage into an approximation of something more human or elvish, occasionally causing the head to detach and form a grotesque, mask-like creature that uses tentacles for locomotion. The parasitic mind is given a taste of its own medicine, and the body, now the victim of two transformative invasions, becomes even less recognizable.

entries.name: Puppet Master
entries: An uncontained psiophage can go from living rent-free in an intellect devourer's head to becoming the landlord of an entire colony. Able to jump from host to host and leave a trail of catatonic victims in its wake, a single psiophage can, if it reaches an elder brain, turn the entire colony into its own personal puppet show. Only one case of this has been documented, but the reports describe an entirely invisible fortress of winding, nonsensical halls wandered by pasty, expressionless tentacle marionettes. The heroes who destroyed it spent the entire sum of their reward money on therapy afterwards.

entries.name: Imitation of Life
entries: Mimeflayers demonstrate a bizarre curiosity for creatures and objects on the physical planes and spend most of their time mimicking things they've seen. They manifest their imitations as invisible, psychic echos, and observers have noted boxes, ropes, doors, and even weapons formed from pure thought. Though possession is no threat to a creature void of psychic talents, a mimeflayer's curiosity can extend to the macabre and grotesque. It is not unusual for a seemingly innocuous and friendly individual to suddenly want to see what happens when an elf is crushed inside a shrinking invisible box.

monsterFluff.name: Painted Lady
entries: {@i Muse of Dark Deeds}
{@b Habitat:} Planar (Lower Planes); {@b Treasure:} Arcana
{@i I pleaded for her not to destroy my home, but she said it was for ‘character development.' Then she started painting with my tears.}

Painted ladies believe that art is born through tragedy, and they're willing to be proactive in creating both. Curators of the Lower Planes, their artistic natures grant them a higher reputation among mortals than their more devious fiendish colleagues, but make no mistake, they craft their works with blood.

entries.name: Symbiotic Muse
entries: The best art is collaborative, and a painted lady seeks out mortals of prowess and potential to serve as underlings. It relishes stories of dark deeds and desperate heroics, and it bestows both power and strife to its fragile followers to facilitate these tales. The sprawling legacy of a painted lady and its pawns is found decorating every surface of its domain, rendered by its supernatural skill.

entries.name: Legend Seeker
entries: Despite their devilish natures, painted ladies are frequently sought out as muses by desperate artists looking to gain renown. Their hopes are spurred on by stories of success, as many of the greatest artists in history were sponsored by a painted lady. Cloud Mounae, Renblundt, and Jay Inke all attribute their success to painted ladies, and all met gruesome ends, often at the hands of competing artists. Unfortunately, these tales of conflict and death don't deter art students from burning paintings and reciting infernal sonnets to the devils in their dormitories after hours.

entries.name: Competitive Inspiration
entries: Painted ladies view conflict as the purest means of progression. They adore competition, bashing their favorite mortal playthings against each other like dolls until one of them breaks. The Academia Inferna of Fine Arts, sponsored by the Lady Cruenta, lasted only a single semester before it was shut down. It turns out that a pencil sketching competition to the death is bad for student retention.

monsterFluff.name: Magnétanque
entries: {@i Living Ore with an Attractive Nature}
{@b Habitat:} Hill, Mountain; {@b Treasure:} None
"{@i We made the new recruits get as close as possible to the magnétanque without it noticing and told them they'd be ‘shocked' by what they saw. The lieutenant didn't think it was funny.}"

If magnétanques had their way, they would live alone in blissful solitude. Unfortunately, hunters, tinkers, and sadists keep trying to find them. Naturally shy creatures, their first instinct is to run when confronted by others, but when cornered or startled (which happens often), they let loose with their natural electromagnetism, shocking culprits, frying gizmos, and rattling any nearby utensils.

entries.name: Magnetic Core
entries: A magnétanque's body is made up of rare earth metals, and their cores are composed of a powerfully magnetic neodymium alloy. As they waddle around, the layers of their bodies rapidly rotate around each other, generating high levels of electrical current. Taking advantage of their electromagnetic nature, they've evolved to have magnetized limbs that are detached from their main body, granting a greater range of motion and outputs for their electrical current.

entries.name: Living Ore
entries: Since observations of the reclusive magnétanque led to breakthroughs in the field of artifice, demand for magnétanque alloy has spiked, leading to a sharp rise in poaching. A hunting subculture has arisen among tinkers and mages, who have designed a slew of wood, bone, and ceramic weapons made specifically to kill and disassemble these energized Elementals.

monsterFluff.name: Bicephal Wyrmhole
entries: {@i Bifurcated Graviturgical Anomaly}
{@b Habitat:} Hill, Mountain; {@b Treasure:} Any
"{@i You see, Albert!? This is why we don't use our sentient magical weapons for rotisserie!}"
entries.by: Archmage Relata Teor on an expedition with her new apprentice

entries: Everyone loves a mystical weapon with strange powers beyond mortal comprehension, but using them always carries an inherent risk. Leave them "sheathed" in the wrong monster overnight, and you end up with a brain-twisting nightmare. This is why we clean our blades, people!

entries.name: Bizarre Fusion
entries: Formed when an aberrant blade binds itself to a wyvern corpse, the draconic body becomes a puppet for the sentient distortion within it. The dark pool of energy at its centre bends the air around it, consuming any light that enters. Between the strange anatomy and the visual warping of reality, many adventurers get disoriented and die trying to sever a head that was really more of an accessory anyway.

entries.name: Odd Armour
entries: Using the gravity well at the core of its body, a bicephal wyrmhole pulls in chips of minerals and metals, slowly replacing the rotting flesh of its host with a durable outer shell. The process, akin to a rapid fossilization, leaves a vaguely metallic and crystalline exterior, which shimmers in odd patterns under the bent light of the Aberration's gravitational field.

entries.name: Distortion Zone
entries: At a distance, a bicephal wyrmhole can be easily mistaken for a mirage or trick of the light, making them hard to discern, but there are several sure signs to indicate one's presence in the area. Barely perceptible shifts in the colouration of nearby objects, making them appear blurry or oversaturated, can be used to confirm a bicephal wyrmhole is nearby. That, and the occasional stand of floating cabbages.

monsterFluff.name: Somnoblin
entries: {@i Fusions of Pure Obsession}
{@b Habitat:} Forest, Grassland, Hill, The Low, Urban; {@b Treasure:} Implements, Individual

While most intertwined are widely regarded as esoteric and wise, one that is brought about without potent enough magic, or with a smaller slice of ambition, turns out... less wise. Unable to possess a creature directly, these frail psyches latch onto objects or tokens, waiting to impart their most obsessive personality trait onto whichever pliable and unlucky creature finds the item.

entries.name: Like-Minded Bond
entries: Strong-willed creatures and individuals that do not share the psyche's principal trait can interact with such a token and leave none-the-wiser to its true potential. However, should the token be found by a creature whose motivation matches that of the psyche, what was once a balanced impulse can become an all-consuming compulsion as a somnoblin. A kobold inventor's fascination with explosives could grow into pyromania, causing it to leave a frivolous trail of destruction in its wake. A paladin's desire to protect can cause them to throw themself before a rolling boulder, heedless to the fact its soon-tobe- flattened body will not save the bunnies down the hill. And a goblin afraid of the limelight might develop the ability to disappear from view the moment it dons the token on its finger.

entries.name: Contagious Compulsion
entries: This newly exacerbated obsession can leach into the surrounding area like a rotting corpse into a water supply. A somnoblin setting up shop in an abandoned cellar or attic can sweep an entire town with an unnatural craze. In Aramachi, an obsession with mycology nearly tanked the region's economy as they farmed nothing but mushrooms, much to the chagrin of the brewers downstream. In Bodrum, an entire city block was painted chartreuse when a somnoblin in the sewers decided that was its favorite colour. Even normal fads often get blamed on somnoblins, leading to the erroneous assumption that teenagers are more susceptible to their powers, despite ample evidence to the contrary.

monsterFluff.name: High Priest of the Astringentum
entries: {@i Acid Fanatic and Metal Despiser}
{@b Habitat:} Desert, Hill, Mountain; {@b Treasure:} Relics

{@i How was my visit to the Abbey of the Astrigentum? Well, I took off all my armour and weapons, but the doorkeeper noticed I had a metal dental filling. My choices were to leave or gargle acid, so no, I didn't get to see inside!}

The astringentum form a peculiar and radical sect among the formigans, attesting that metal is the root of all evil in the world. Those sympathetic to the cause avoid and detest anything metallic, and it is the high clergy's raison d'être to rid the world of it. They claim that, once it's all destroyed, the world will be restored to a perfect state.

entries.name: Caustic Disciples
entries: A formigan's dish can be used for many purposes—carrying things, defense, and even the production of useful substances. The high priests of the astringentum sacrifice all other options to maximize what they view as a divine calling: the power to secrete acid. Producing a type strong enough to melt through metal is a sure sign of destiny for the astringentum, and those with the gift are trained until they can do nothing else with their dishes.

entries.name: Acrid Blessings
entries: To the devout in the congregation— those who can withstand the acidic burning— an outpouring of holy liquid from a high priest can grant various boons. Followers claim it extends life, purifies the body, and quickens the wits. Most people are unaware of these benefits, as they're too distracted by the awful smell to listen.

entries.name: Dissolution
entries: Each decade, the high priests lead a dissolution pilgrimage, in which their caravan travels from town to town destroying any metal they encounter. When an inevitably angry mob of toolless farmers opposes this holy tradition, the high priests quench any violence, using their acid to empower followers and burn enemies. The worst of these conflicts was the crusade against the dwarves of the Iron Maiden clan, who revere heavy metal as much as the astringentum hates it. The battles persisted long after the pilgrimage would have ceased, and they only ended after the revered smith, Pep Taw, crafted the legendaryBis-Maul, which neutralized the clergy's caustic characteristics.

monsterFluff.name: Stonemaw
entries: {@i The Teeth Beneath the Sands}
{@b Habitat:} Desert, Hill, Mountain; {@b Treasure:} Arcana (Gemstones only)
{@i Let's try over there! That circle of pointed stones looks like the perfect spot to set up camp.}
The stonemaw is a simple creature that, if it could talk, would swear by its own perfected technique: open your mouth in the place where the food is walking and wait for your dinner.

entries.name: Lurker in the Ground
entries: Stonemaws, being ambush predators, are exceptionally patient hunters. A stonemaw starts tunneling up to a mile away from a road, animal path, water hole—any area that might be frequented by its prey. After burrowing with adroit stealth beneath its chosen spot, it extends its jaw, pushing the tips of its teeth above the surface. The moment a potential meal crosses the waiting mouth, SNAP!

entries.name: Gravity Field
entries: To hide both their tunnels and their massive bulk, stonemaws have developed a unique relationship with gravity. The area within a couple inches of a stonemaw has a strong gravitational repulsion. This force not only serves to keep the stonemaw light on its feet but also assists in burrowing. Rather than ripping stone and soil away with its claws, the earth is simply pushed and compressed out of the way, falling back into place as the stonemaw passes, leaving little trace that it was there at all.

entries.name: Mineral Supplements
entries: Though zoologists would definitively classify the stonemaw as a carnivorous beast, it relies on more than meat to survive. Each time it feeds, it ingests large amounts of sediment, which its body refines to reinforce its teeth, scales, and other protrusions that cover its body. Some arcanists posit that this makes it a demi-elemental, and the Council of Original Zoological Ideas and Etymological Stipulations has wallowed in indecision for years.

monsterFluff.name: Obakitsu Grandmaster
entries: {@i Instructor in Death}
{@b Habitat:} Mountain, Planar (Ethereal Plane), Urban; {@b Treasure:} Armaments

{@i Everything I've taught you has been the art of combat. The sweeping taught you the movements of the staff, the roof tiling taught you the weight of the hammer, and the reinvestment of my life insurance funds taught you... well, most of what I've taught you has been the art of combat.}

Kitsune dedicate their lives to learning and mastering their skills over centuries, eventually passing their vast knowledge and wisdom down to the next generation. Despite their incredible longevity, a few still find the time too short. When a kitsune master of the martial arts is dissatisfied with the brevity of their mortality, they may continue teaching in spirit as an obakitsu.

entries.name: Skill Sentinel
entries: Obakitsu are the subject of legends, and heroes will go to incredible lengths to receive tutelage from one of these masters. Techniques that are dead to the wider world of mortals remain sharp in the tails of an obakitsu, and their skill with any given weapon is rarely rivaled in the lands of the living.

entries.name: Trial of Worthiness
entries: Only the most honourable warriors are deemed worthy to train under an obakitsu, and each must prove this worthiness to a prospective master through combat. An aspiring student must best the obakitsu's fox form, a nimble warrior that holds the weapons of failed challengers in its maw. On the rare instances an obakitsu is bested, the proper etiquette of success is to tidy the space in patient preparation for the sensei to return in their humanoid form and begin instruction of weapon masteries lost to time.

entries.name: Spirit Teacher
entries: In extremely rare cases, a warrior may impress an obakitsu enough to earn a position as an apprentice, receiving a larger body of knowledge over years of repeated visits. This specialized training often culminates in the apprentice being introduced to the obakitsu's collection of weapons on the Ethereal Plane and choosing one as a symbol of their mastery.

monsterFluff.name: Purrmafrost
entries: {@i Lethal Snow Frolicker}
{@b Habitat:} Arctic; {@b Treasure:} Armaments
{@i Aww, aren't you the cutest little thing! What are you chewing on there? Is that my leg? Aww, is it tas—wait, that's my leg! Why can't I feel my leg?!}

Don't let those big sparkly eyes and adorable little paws fool you. The purrmafrost is among the deadliest predators ever recorded. Those cutesy traits make it a ruthless predator, and once it starts stalking its prey, there's no escape. This sweet kitty has a ninety-nine percent success rate, even if most of those kills are snow moths.

entries.name: Illusion Hunters
entries: Most prey never catch a glimpse of their feline assailant, as the purrmafrost uses its frosty coat to stir up a fog as soon as a meal is in sight. The fearsome hunter crouches low in the fog while projecting the illusion of a weak, helpless kitten—the perfect snack for a cold, hungry beast. The unwary prey follows the illusion as it limps directly into a freezing trap.

entries.name: Best Served Cold
entries: Beyond the cover it provides, a purrmafrost's fog leaves a layer of moisture on everything it touches, soaking into clothes, skin, fur, and hair. This dampness is key to the purrmafrost's secret weapon. Its breath weapon isn't much on its own, but adding a bit of water to the mix can freeze creatures solid in an instant. This leaves the purrmafrost free to make a killing blow against the newly frozen ice sculpture and drag its prize back to its den for a victory feast.

entries.name: Lethally Cute
entries: When the purrmafrost was discovered, trappers brought it back to sell as a cute and exotic pet. The frosty kitties were banned in every major city within the year, as neighbours complained about missing cats, dogs, livestock, and horses. The reports all shared the same calling card: a splatter of blood and the cold dew of a wintry mist.

monsterFluff.name: Bladeborne Mantiroar
entries: {@i Forlorn, Embattled Hero}
{@b Habitat:} Desert, Grassland, Swamp; {@b Treasure:} Armaments

{@i War. War never changes. Aren't you tired of it? Tired of swinging your tail to send the divine Volley of a Thousand Needles at the corrupted Lords of the Deep and their sinister Legions of Shadow? We all know the same struggle.}

Created by forgotten warlords of the Upper Planes during the Epoch of Blood, these stalwart soldiers are the last vestiges of an ancient war against darkness. Thousands of noble warriors perished in the conflict, but those who survived were left to wander with nothing but their lust for battle, crusading against whatever was in the vicinity, from demons to dragonflies.

entries.name: Golden Battalion
entries: Mantiroars possess a divine gift—the power to conjure radiant weapons with naught but a thought. These golden armaments burned through countless enemies in their glory days, but hopelessness and time have decayed them to mere shadows of what they once were. This despair leaches into the land itself, turning the ground to ash and degrading the steel and bronze of other arms. Despite this dark outlook, a bladeborne mantiroar will still fight tooth and claw and hoof and sword and lance and javelin in any form of conflict.

entries.name: Built for War
entries: Every part of a bladeborne mantiroar's anatomy is designed for battle: hooves like hammers, tails like lances, and fur made of tiny, excruciatingly-grown blades. Historians and biologists have long argued about where mantiroars get the metal to produce their weapons-grade fur. The leading theory is that mantiroars eat weapons as food, though no researchers have lived to report this behaviour.

entries.name: Living Relic
entries: During the Epoch of Blood, mantiroars were both feared and respected, but that perception deteriorated along with the warriors, slipping away until the monstrous soldiers were all but forgotten. After centuries, the few who still remembered began preaching of their glory, building a devoted cult of worshippers who believed these lost instruments of the gods would herald in a new golden age. Embarking on a grand pilgrimage, they bowed before their idols, offering tithes of weapons and gold. Their praises turned into screams as they were massacred en masse, leaving the mantiroars to drift off into obscurity once more.

monsterFluff.name: Reef Avatar
entries: {@i Living Marine Refuge}
{@b Habitat:} Coastal, Underwater; {@b Treasure:} Any
{@i I met one while swimming along the Erdic Coast and convinced one of the miniature orcas to come home with me in a bucket. It... didn't stay mini}

Born as a defense mechanism when oceanic ecosystems are threatened, reef avatars are collective masses of tiny organisms that coalesce into much larger entities. They plod along the ocean floor, taking refugee critters into their twisting tunnels as they work to mend damage to their homes.

entries.name: Restoring Presence
entries: A reef avatar's biology extends beyond what would generally be called its "body." A cloud of larval coral fills the water surrounding the avatar, warning the main body of incoming dangers and latching onto the bones of bleached reefs, where the avatar uses its magic to accelerate their growth into new coral structures. The spontaneity of these growths keeps eagleray-eyed spotters in high demand; from the roc's nest atop a ship's mast, they keep watch for unmapped reefs that may have grown overnight. It's considered awful luck to shatter a reef—their avatars often come looking for revenge.

entries.name: Palace in Miniature
entries: In its wanderings, a reef avatar can come across aquatic denizens forced from their homes by ecological decay. Using its magic, the avatar shrinks them down to fit within its own ecosystem, where they thrive within its rigid, coral skeleton. The avatar creates whichever oceanic biomes of various salinities and temperatures it needs in order to care for the creatures it carries.

entries.name: Symbiotic Defenders
entries: Content to travel in their new temporary home, the sea life that lives within the avatar stands ready to defend it should its home come under threat. Swarms of sharks and squads of cetaceans emerge to drive off wouldbe predators, supported by healing magic from their sentient home. On occasion, an aquatic Humanoid will accept a role in the internal world of the avatar's body, shrinking down to act as a steward and caretaker for the hidden world of creatures within.

monsterFluff.name: Embroidered Voidspawn Cloak
entries: {@i Dreaded Fashion Statement}
{@b Habitat:} Any; {@b Treasure:} None
{@i Please don't kill me! I swear I didn't mean it when I called you tacky! You're actually really stylish in a vintage, ‘I don't care what people think about me' kind of way.}

The allure of ancient, unknowable powers is formidable, but the repercussions are implacable and permanent. Voidspawn cloaks wouldn't exist if it weren't for the meddling of wizards and fashion designers, but meddle they did, and now entire towns are engulfed in starlight and madness because someone wanted a statement piece.

entries.name: Blood of the Ancients
entries: In a bygone time, ancient wizards and arcane runway models imbued their cloaks and robes with the potent ichor of a long dead god from the outer realms of madness. No one batted an eyelid when the robes first moved on their own, and it wasn't until they started turning into monsters that the arcanists decided the powers weren't worth the potential cost. To protect the world, they stashed the remaining clothing in the darkest, least accessible location they could find: the weird, hard to reach back corners of their closets.

entries.name: Maddening Couture
entries: The mind of a dead god is already far beyond most understanding, so cloth made sentient via the decomposing blood of said dead god is somehow even more inscrutable. Granted the gift of life, these ponderous creatures sail across the landscape aimlessly, filling the night with stars and horror, clothing the poor people who encounter it with its nebulous fabric. Those draped in these strange folds are driven mad by fashion beyond mortal comprehension.

entries.name: Cut of the Cloth
entries: While the original ritual used to create the cloaks was thankfully lost to time, it's suspected that the mature creatures can reproduce somehow. Legends and songs state that the original robes were a limited-edition, serialized run, but there have been far more incidents than the serial numbers suggest. The leading theory is that a mature voidspawn cloak can cut parts of itself off to leave as cloaks on lost-and-found racks and in secondhand stores, waiting for some unsuspecting hero to start wearing it around.

monsterFluff.name: Necropod
entries: {@i Eldritch Prophet in the Deep}
{@b Habitat:} Ocean; {@b Treasure:} Arcana

{@i Extractink blood from zis thing alvays results in ein sanguine cocktail of its last few victims. Sehr frustratink. Zis hast never been ein problem vit mein own samples, even after ein large meal...}

Despite the best efforts of the Borderless Union of Magizoological Sciences, sailors and fishermen the world over continue to call necropods by the gross misnomer "vampires of the deep". Admittedly, these creatures do drain the blood from their victims, leaving soggy, salted husks floating in the surf—but that's where the similarities end. Necropods syphon blood through specialised tentacles rather than a bite, and their billowing silhouettes are the result of flared upper fins rather than overly dramatic capes.

entries.name: Bloodmonger
entries: Necropods don't consume blood for sustenance—their actual diet consists of nutrients and minerals absorbed from fields of rich deep-sea vents. The lifeblood they drain is instead offered as a sacrifice to otherworldly forces beyond mortal comprehension. In exchange, the necropods are granted fleeting visions of the future, surges of unnatural vitality, and powerful necrotic magic.

entries.name: Dark Watchers
entries: The stronger a creature's life force, the deeper a necropod can peer into the branching threads of possibility. This drives them to hunt relentlessly for strong prey, favouring those with rich life experience, unshakeable willpower, and long-term exposure to magic—qualities that mark adventurers as their most coveted quarry. Why necropods fixate so fervently on their blood-bought visions remains shrouded in esoteric mystery... but sailors' superstition claims that, on the eve of calamitous events, these creatures gather in vast shoals in the lightless depths.

entries.name: Adapted for Terror
entries: Evidence of necropods dates back millenia, and biomancers have long debated the origin of their appearance—did these creatures change naturally to mimic squids and octopuses, or did they somehow shape the development of cephalopods in their own image? In recent years, research into these Aberrations has brought forth a third theory: that the necropods saw deep sea monsters in the minds of their victims and deliberately manipulated their own evolution to imitate the fears of their prey.

monsterFluff.name: Sea Giant Jotunbjerg
entries: {@i Frosty Wanderers Far from the Peaks}
{@b Habitat:} Arctic, Coast, Ocean; {@b Treasure:} Armaments
"{@i Sing, o skald, of the lost children of ice, who gave us our home beneath the waves! Sing, o skald, of the first to descend, and all those who followed! Sing, o skald, in praise}"
entries.by: Hymn of the First of the Sea

entries: Once, the songs of the sea giants rolled across every coast, shaking the cliffs and beaches with their rumbling bass... and then the krakens came. In the wake of the Sjøhjem massacre, both the giants and their hymns all but vanished, scattered to solitary settlements in the ocean's deepest trenches. Nowadays, one fortunate enough to hear a sea giant's guttural melodies should strive to appreciate them at a brisk run in the opposite direction— for they herald a vengeful, bloody kraken hunt.

entries.name: The Descent
entries: The sea giants trace their origins to the mountaintop monastery of Hoytkloster, which their frost giant predecessors inhabited during the Great War of Thunder. Though these uncharacteristically peaceful giants chose to withdraw from the conflict, the war found them nonetheless, shattering their sanctuary and hurling them into the icy abyss of the ocean below. There, the giants wove ancient magic to reshape their bodies for this new environment, trading white capped peaks for white capped waves. Not only adapting but thriving, they forged a glorious kingdom in the depths, raising the sunken monastery into the grand capital of Sjøhjem.

entries.name: Silenced Songs
entries: The peace of their new home wasn't to last, as the resonating hymns of the sea giants carried across the depths to the earholes of envious krakens. Seething with spite for the invaders from above the waves, the betentacled sea titans ripped apart the fledgling giant cities in a devastating massacre. The few survivors were left to rebuild in silence, shackled by fear—to this day, sea giant communities still suppress the joyful songs that once defined them, lest they draw the krakens' ire anew. Only the bravest and most wrathful among them dare to reject this indignity, hunting their sworn nemeses in the hopes that their people may one day join their voices in harmony once more.

entries.name: Ascendant Hunters
entries: In order to avoid fighting krakens in the crushing depths of their underwater lairs, where they're at their most dangerous, the giant hunters devised a cunning strategy to lure their foes to land. Upon approaching a kraken's lair, all but one giant ascend to the surface on their buoyant ice until they vanish from sight. The lone decoy lingers below, intoning somber, half-remembered hymns from the hollows of its chest to needle the kraken's ancient spite. When the mighty titan surges from its nest in a blind fury, hoping for an easy kill, the giant rockets upward, drawing its prey to the ambush above.

monsterFluff.name: Deep Gazer
entries: {@i Ravenous Lights in the Dark}
{@b Habitat:} Ocean; {@b Treasure:} Any
"{@i Such pretty lights. Some mage is sending us a message, a plea for help with his cantrips. Surely there's no harm in checking. We... we should go over there... yeah... pretty lights...}"

Twinkling lights dance in the pitch black water, playful and enticing, but seasoned sailors know better than to be lured by the mesmerising display. Perhaps it could be treasure this time, yes... but no amount of gold is worth the risk of wandering into a deep gazer's lair. Never trust the lights in the darkest places of the world.

entries.name: Creatures of Madness
entries: The distinctive anatomy of deep gazers—with several eyestalk-like lures arranged around a large, central eye—marks them unmistakably as the devolved kin of eye tyrants. As it turns out, not even these fearsome Aberrations can withstand the mutagenic madness of the Voidsea indefinitely. In time, the ceaseless tide of corruption causes them to regress into a more primitive, bestial state, driven not by an eye tyrant's usual schemes and paranoia, but raw hunger alone.

entries.name: Glimmer in the Dark
entries: As the mind of an eye tyrant succumbs to the Voidsea, its body followssuit, rapidly reshaping itself to suit its new environment. Its many eyes, useless in the lightless abyss, become glowing lures with which a deep gazer draws its prey—and through which it channels powerful enchantment magic in place of eye rays. Even those that recognise the danger nonetheless find their limbs betraying them, inching ever closer to the blade-like teeth of the gazer's maw and the suffocating gaze of its one remaining eye.

entries.name: Dreamcatchers
entries: A deep gazer's lures draw in more than prey. Likely a remnant from before their descent into madness, these gazers lure in the hopes, dreams, and memories of nearby creatures. These fragments of thought float through the water near the oblivious gazer, drifting until they fade into obscurity. Those who survive a tussle with a deep gazer sometimes recollect things they'd forgotten long ago, or even "remember" tales and experiences from people who died centuries before.

monsterFluff.name: Chuulverin
entries: {@i Lieutenants of Lobsteresque Legions}
{@b Habitat:} Ocean; {@b Treasure:} None

"{@i Not many people can say they've survived being shot from a chuulverin's cannon. I'll admit, I don't remember much after I crashed headfirst through the enemy's hull, but the concussion was almost worth the bragging rights}"

The sight of even a single chuulverin's cannon rising up from the water's surface is a terrible omen, for it tells all present that the abolethian armies have committed everything to the oncoming assault. These rare and mighty specimens are deployed only sparingly by their masters— generally when a battle must be bloody, swift, and utterly crushing.

entries.name: Ancient Artillery
entries: When the aboleths sought to usurp the gods, the chitin-plated chuulverin stood among their most valuable weapons. Acting in the capacity of military commanders as much as living siege weapons, these crustacean colossi rasped orders to the lobsteresque legions from the rear while unleashing volleys of sulphurous artillery upon the battlefield. Under a chuulverin's command, all crumbled before the relentless advance of the chitinous swarm—iron hulls, fortress walls, and eventually, the resolve of any who opposed them.

entries.name: Smoky Gestation
entries: Though aboleths seed seas and caverns across the world with nests of chuul eggs, only those buried near volcanic vents on the ocean floor give rise to the dreaded chuulverin. There, protected by crushing ressure and shrouded in the boiling darkness, the eggs steep in plumes of mineral-rich smoke. A chuulverin requires a full year of gestation before it finally crawls forth from its deep-sea crucible, wreathed in a metal-infused shell and endowed with living artillery fit to sink fleets for its aboleth masters.

entries.name: Organic Ordnance
entries: The bony protrusion on a chuulverin's back resembles a cannon in both form and function—only instead of powder and shot, it contains a portal to the depths in which the creature was born. Through this portal, the chuulverin draws superheated water under tremendous pressure, launching it in powerful jets that shatter wooden hulls and pulverise stone walls. Occasionally, a mature chuul egg is dragged through the intake and violently hatched on impact.

monsterFluff.name: Draconimbus
entries: {@i Cursed Fury of the Storm}
{@b Habitat:} Arctic/Coast/Underwater; {@b Treasure:} Implements

"{@i None can tell ya which head'll bite, so stash yer steel and put out the light. Wrap yer bones to keep ‘em warm, and cover yer ears against the storm. Close yer eyes and pray fer the light, cause none can tell ya which head'll bite.}"

Death in a Voidsea storm comes in four terrifying flavours. Ask any sailor, and they'll tell you without hesitation which is the best way to die—though you'll rarely see them agree. Rowdy tavern brawls over opinions on the draconimbus have made for many a black eye and lost tooth... but it beats fighting one of these terrifying Elementals by far.

entries.name: Sentient Storm
entries: During particularly vicious tempests, even the natives of the Elemental Planes are powerless to resist the maddening winds and warping tides of the Voidsea. When several air and water elementals are swept up and violently churned together in the swirling hurricanes at the heart of a storm, they fuse into a single abomination: a four-headed, elemental hydra known as a draconimbus. These oceanic terrors embody the storm in its totality, and delight in unleashing their fury against the ships and souls caught in their path.

entries.name: The Four Winds
entries: Each of a draconimbus's four heads channels one of the aspects of the storm. Lightning lances through masts and burns away flesh. Thunder deafens sailors and shatters hulls. Freezing rain lashes the body, leeching warmth. And winds carry the whispering madness of the depths across the deck, whipping insanity into all who hear it. Alone, each could break the resolve of a hardened crew... together, they can unmake entire fleets.

entries.name: Stormwardens
entries: The threat of these sentient storms is not to be underestimated. Larger vessels often avoid setting sail without the protection of one or more stormwardens—seasoned mages specialised in elemental manipulation, capable of binding even the treacherous winds and tides of the Voidsea to their will. These mercenaries help ships skirt the tempestuous territories of draconimbi and, should one of these creatures catch the vessel off-guard, might even be able to keep them at bay long enough to escape... though most would rather never have to try.
{@i Note to self: when playing with a meowsoon, disregard local weather divinations. Even when inside.}

entries.name: Meowsoon
entries: {@b Type:} Elemental
{@b Creature Component:} Any Draconimbus
{@b Bonus Tamer Improvement:} Rainy Day and +2 {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Dice}

Born from storm clouds saturated with raw primordial energy, the playful meowsoons trail titans of elemental might—krakens, dragon turtles, even the sky-darkening draconimbuses—all across the Voidsea, gleefully frolicking in the tempestuous playgrounds left in their wake. These endearing antics have earned them the moniker "storm chasers" from the Climate Location and Observation United Division, a fledgling order of atmomancers and diviners devoted to providing accurate weather predictions planewide. Tamers who bond with these friendly felines are advised to carry an umbrella at all times... as well as rubber boots, waterproof cloaks, and a heavy object with which to anchor themselves—just in case.
If any of the meowsoon's traits or actions require a saving throw, it is always against the meowsoon's save DC, as calculated below.

entries.name: Save DC
entries.text: 8 + the Tamer's Proficiency Bonus + the meowsoon's Dexterity modifier

entries.name: Monster Trainer
entries: If you are a Tamer, you can grant this familiar the following improvements using your Monster Trainer feature in addition to those normally available for your companions.

entries.name: Rainy Day
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Become a Tamer's companion. Type: Passive (Companion), Active (Bonus Action)}.

The meowsoon can take a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} to amplify the weather in a 5-foot {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} centred on itself, filling the area with heavy rain. Whenever a creature enters the area or starts its turn there, it becomes Wet. The {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} lasts until the meowsoon uses this {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} again, ends the effect (no action required) or becomes {@condition Unconscious|XPHB}.

entries.name: Storm Out
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 3+ Tamer. Type: Active (Reaction)}.

When an enemy the meowsoon can see ends its turn within 5 feet of it, the meowsoon can take a Reaction to fly up to half its {@variantrule Fly Speed|XPHB} without provoking {@action Opportunity Attacks|XPHB}.

entries.name: Multiattack
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 5+ Tamer. Type: Active (Action)}.
As an action, the meowsoon can make two attacks.

entries.name: Lightning Step I
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 5+ Tamer. Type: Active (Action)}.

As an action, the meowsoon can turn into a bolt of lightning and move up to 60 feet in a straight line without provoking {@action Opportunity Attacks|XPHB}. During this movement, the meowsoon can move through the spaces of other creatures. Each creature whose space the meowsoon moves through makes a Dexterity saving throw, taking 14 ({@damage 4d6}) Lightning damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.
Once the meowsoon uses this action, it can't use it again until its Tamer finishes a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short} or {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.
When the meowsoon's Tamer reaches level 9 in the {@class Tamer|HelianasGuidetoMonsterHunting} class, this damage increases to 21 ({@damage 6d6}).

entries.name: Windy Day
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 5+ Tamer, Rainy Day. Type: Passive (Companion), Active (Bonus Action)}.

The size of the {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} created by the meowsoon's Rainy Day improvement increases to 10 feet. Additionally, when the meowsoon uses this {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB}, it can choose to summon strong winds instead of heavy rain.

If the meowsoon summons strong winds, it doesn't gain the normal benefits of its Rainy Day improvement. Instead, its Fly Speed increases by 20 feet, and creatures in the {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} have Half Cover against ranged attacks.

entries.name: Conductive
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 9+ Tamer. Type: Passive (Companion)}.

Whenever the meowsoon deals Lightning damage to a {@condition Wet|HFN2025} creature, it can also deal Lightning damage equal to one roll of one of its {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Dice} to a different creature that it can see within 10 feet of the first creature. The Wet condition then ends for the first creature.

entries.name: Growth II
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 9+ Tamer. Type: Passive (Companion)}.

The meowsoon's size increases to Medium, its {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Die} size increases to a d8 (its {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Points maximum} consequently increases by 1 for each of its {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Dice}), and the damage dice of its Voltaiclaw attack increases to a d8.

entries.name: Strong Winds
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 9+ Tamer. Type: Active (Action)}.
The meowsoon gains a new action option: Squall.

{@b Squall.} {@actSave str}: (DC 8 + Con + PB), one creature the meowsoon can see within 30 feet that the meowsoon can see. Failure: 5 ({@damage 2d6}) Thunder damage, or 7 ({@damage 2d6} + Con) Thunder damage if the meowsoon is currently summoning strong winds with its Windy Day improvement. If the target is inside the {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} created by the meowsoon's Rainy Day improvement, the target is pushed up to 15 feet straight away from the meowsoon.
The damage of this action increases by one die when the meowsoon's Tamer reaches levels 13 (three dice) and 17 (four dice) in the Tamer class.

entries.name: Static Clouds
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 13+ Tamer. Type: Passive (Companion)}.

Immediately after the meowsoon is subjected to Lightning damage, it gains Temporary {@variantrule Temporary Hit Points|XPHB} equal to its Constitution modifier (minimum of 1 {@variantrule Temporary Hit Points|XPHB|Temporary Hit Point}).

entries.name: Stormy Day
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 13+ Tamer, Windy Day. Type: Passive (companion), Active (Bonus Action)}.

The size of the {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} created by the meowsoon's Rainy Day improvement increases to 15 feet. Additionally, when the meowsoon uses this {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB}, it can choose to summon thunder storms instead of the other options.

When the meowsoon summons thunder storms, it doesn't gain the normal benefits of its Rainy Day improvement. Instead, it has {@variantrule Immunity|XPHB} to Lightning damage, and a creature in the {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} that hits the meowsoon with an attack roll takes Lightning damage equal to one roll of one of the meowsoon's {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Dice}. A creature can take this damage only once per turn.

entries.name: Lightning Step II
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 13+ Tamer, Lightning Step I. Type: Active (Action)}.

The damage of the meowsoon's Lightning Step I improvement increases to 24 ({@damage 7d6}). In addition, at the start of the meowsoon's next turn after using this action, it creates a shockwave in a 15-foot {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} originating from it. Each creature in the area makes a Constitution saving throw, taking 10 ({@damage 3d6}) Thunder damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one. The shockwave's thunderous sound can be heard up to 100 feet away.
When the meowsoon's Tamer reaches level 17 in the Tamer class, the Lightning damage increases to 28 ({@damage 8d6}) and the Thunder damage increases to 14 ({@damage 4d6}).

entries.name: Hurricane Season
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 17+ Tamer, Stormy Day. Type: Active (Bonus Action)}.

As a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB}, the meowsoon summons heavy rain, strong winds, and thunder storms as per its Rainy Day, Windy Day, and Stormy Day improvements for 1 minute or until it becomes Incapacitated. For the duration, it gains the following benefits.

items.name: Category 5
entries: The meowsoon's size increases to Gargantuan, and the size of the {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} created by its Rainy Day improvement increases to 30 feet.

items.name: Hurricane Form
entries: The meowsoon can enter a creature's space and stop there. The first time it enters a creature's space on a turn, that creature takes 5 ({@damage 1d10}) Bludgeoning damage.

items.name: Storm Incarnate
entries: When the meowsoon uses this {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} and as a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} on subsequent rounds, it can create one of the following effects:

items.name: Eye of the Storm
entries: Each creature in the meowsoon's space must succeed on a Strength saving throw or be Grappled by the meowsoon (escape DC equals meowsoon's save DC). A creature Grappled in this way is Restrained and takes 9 ({@damage 2d8}) Bludgeoning damage at the start of each of the meowsoon's turns.

items.name: Thunderbolt and Lightning
entries: The meowsoon makes a ranged attack (attack modifier equals Dex + PB) against a creature inside its {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation}. On a hit, the target takes 14 ({@damage 4d6}) Lightning damage and is Frightened of the meowsoon until the end of the meowsoon's next turn.

items.name: Wind Currents
entries: The meowsoon attempts to move up to three creatures it can see inside its {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation}. Each creature must succeed on a Strength saving throw or be pushed 30 feet in a straight line in a direction of the meowsoon's choice.
Once the meowsoon activates this improvement, it can't do so again until its Tamer finishes a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

monsterFluff.name: Pengkin Rimejarl
entries: {@i Dapper Trapper on Thin Ice}
{@b Habitat:} Arctic/Coast/Underwater; {@b Treasure:} Relic

"{@i Oh, I assure you the ingredients and pelts are my primary motivation. You haven't lived until you've sampled aberrant cuisine. Keeping the locals safe from monsters is just a convenient bonus. Call it symbiotic.}"

Pengkin rimejarls are unabashed hedonists, indulging in the finer things in life even at the expense of their own health—and sanity. Aberrant delicacies such as aboleth caviar and necropod fin soup may slowly liquify the eater's brains, but to a true gourmand, such trifling inconveniences are a small price to pay for the privilege of an exclusive degustation.

entries.name: Eldritch Eatery
entries: The feast halls of rimejarls are often decorated with all manner of pelts, trophies, and treasures, but appearances can be deceiving— the gluttonous connoisseurs hunt for food first, and everything else a distant second. They devote their lives to experiencing every bounty the sea can offer, from the common to the grotesquely exotic, with a particular fondness for the flesh of Aberrations. Such a diet brings more than just flavour, and rimejarls soon develop the ability to tap into the eldritch power of the creatures they consume. These otherworldly talents are then promptly wielded to track and hunt even more Aberrations... and the cycle continues.

entries.name: Hunting Party
entries: A rimejarl is nothing without its hunting party. To improve their chances of procuring their beloved delicacies, these pengkin surround themselves with skilled hunters from every walk of life. Master rangers track their quarries, grizzled helmsmen manoeuvre their ships, and silvertongued bards embellish their exploits with after-dinner ballads. A wanderer with useful abilities can easily find a hearty welcome in a rimejarl's abode.

entries.name: Whispers on the Wind
entries: Sadly, the decadent flavours of the depths come with significant drawbacks. The more eldritch delicacies a rimejarl consumes—and the more it relies on the magic they confer—the more prone it becomes to severe paranoia and bouts of bizarre hubris. Many hunting parties end in tragic bloodbaths when a rimejarl's delusions escalate minor disagreements into violent altercations. In fact, pengkin historical records show that rimejarls almost always die in one of two ways: at the hands of a meal or the hands of a friend.

monsterFluff.name: Soul Hoarder
entries: {@i Shady Collector with a Keen Eye}
{@b Habitat:} Mountain, Swamp, The Low, Urban; {@b Treasure:} Any
"{@i Let it be a mint-condition dwarven wizard, or maybe a limited edition gnome barbarian... Ugh. Another human fighter.}"
entries.by: The Soul Hoarder, Raiding for Collectibles

entries: Every dragon has its pile of treasure. To the legendary Soul Hoarder, this treasure is not gold, but rare creatures of singular talent or exquisite beauty. The more storied an individual, the more bizarre its abilities, the more endangered its species, the more the Soul Hoarder craves it, needs it. And once a specimen catches its eye, the Hoarder will stop at nothing to see it added to its ever-expanding collection, perfectly preserved for all eternity.

entries.name: Freakish Display
entries: Enchanted vials, bottles, and jars keep the Hoarder's collection healthy and visible, each specimen miniaturised for display on the winding shelves of its macabre home. It organizes along themed criteria, rearranging as necessary if a new idea occurs to it. The three-legged quadrupeds are kept separate from the much rarer three-legged bipeds, and the non-pigmented section takes up an entire cavern with its pale shelves.

entries.name: Multiplanar Tracker
entries: After centuries of research in cramped caverns, the Soul Hoarder has lost its power of flight, exchanging it for arcane skill. It leverages this skill to drift in and out of the Ethereal Plane, surveying the world unseen in search of unique souls warped by powerful magic—warlocks, sorcerers, and time travelers are all coveted pieces for both study and display.

entries.name: Trading Creature Game
entries: Though adventurers who face it must inevitably contend with the rank and file of its menagerie, the Soul Hoarder is nothing if not a savvy collector and investor. Its rarest—and often most powerful—specimens seldom see the light of day for fear of damage or reduction in market value. How exactly this value is determined, or who buys the hapless creatures, is a mystery to everyone but the Soul Hoarder, but it proudly insists that it offers only fair trades. Whenever an exotic monster disappears from the dragon's curated displays, it's usually in exchange for powerful adventurers or high ranking nobility.

monsterFluff.name: Briar Lord
entries: {@i Woodland Guardians in Perfect Sync}
{@b Habitat:} Forest, Grassland, Jungle, Swamp; {@b Treasure:} Armament
"{@i Your careless footsteps have carried you to the heart of the Divine Grove. The council will now decide your fate.}"
entries.by: The Maple Triumvirate, Speaking in Unison

entries: Briar lords put the "blade" in blade of grass. As arbiters of the law of the forest, they judge the worthiness of all who enter the sacred spaces hidden deep within the woods. Those deemed unworthy face a final trial by combat.

entries.name: Vassals of Trees
entries: Each briar lord serves under one or more treants, acting under their command to safeguard the forest from intrusion. Though not as powerful as the mighty shepherds of the forest, the lords' unflinching devotion has earned them the honour of defending the woods' most sacred sites. An ancient tree planted by a goddess, an enchanted pool that reflects the past, or a stand of spruces dedicated to druidic rituals may all fall under the vigilant watch of these nimble guardians.

entries.name: Blades of Grass
entries: A briar lord in the forest is never unarmed—the verdant expanse offers itself freely as an armoury to its protectors. Simple branches twist themselves into razor sharp edges in a lord's grasp, while sap drawn from any tree grows toxic at their will. Though even the smallest leaf can be deadly in their hands, most lords prefer polearms that blend in with high branches and can reach down to strike from above.

entries.name: Lords' Council
entries: In more vital locations, a single briar lord may prove insufficient—a council of three is thus formed to ensure the success of their sacred duties. Each member of the council surveys the site separately, observing all they can before convening to determine the fate of any intruders by majority. By the time they reveal themselves to trespassers, a verdict has already been passed, and all that is left is to carry out the sentencing.

monsterFluff.name: Grand Maestro Sangrimm
entries: {@i Ringleader of the Dance of Death}
{@b Habitat:} The Low, Urban; {@b Treasure:} Relics

"{@i Life and death are partners in the great dance of time. Each perfect step they take, each flawless turn, each hypnotising twirl… they will last in memory long after the music stops. This legacy of mine will outlast my withering flesh and flame.}"

Sangrimm's traveling performance, the Dance of Death, is an interactive show that invites participants to gamble their vitality away for a shot at fame and glory. His challenges offer unimaginable boons to those who win… all at the mere cost of a week or two off the end of their lives.

entries.name: Eternal Showman
entries: Sangrimm has peddled his show across the planes for centuries. He opens each performance claiming that it could be his last, that his life is fleeting, and that the dance must soon come to a halt. Dramatic speeches and morbid predictions of his own death are undercut by the fact that he shows no signs of aging or decay, leading to countless rumours that the vitality he extracts as payment from his hopeful contestants is used to prolong his own lifespan.

entries.name: Popular Pariah
entries: Sangrimm has built quite the fan club among every demographic… with the notable exception of other thanatophobia jinn, who see his antics as a desperate and radical deviation from tradition. For many, this disapproval is but a way to mask their jealousy of Sangrimm's clever scheme, which they condemn for its reckless reliance on chance. What they don't understand, of course, is that Sangrimm rigs the show so that he always comes out on top.

monsterFluff.name: Hivebound Champion
entries: {@i Sticky Team in a Steel Suit}
{@b Habitat:} Forest, Jungle, Swamp; {@b Treasure:} Armament

"{@i I jumped right in, hacking, slashing, and ripping my opponent apart. I should've known I was cutting through the armour too cleanly. By the time I'd realized my mistake, the little guys had me surrounded.}"

Three kobolds in a trenchcoat can only dream of achieving the level of teamwork and cooperation shown by a hivebound champion. Though easily mistaken for a single—if somewhat inelegant—creature, the knight's "body" is actually composed of several small Oozes, each piloting a different portion of the armoured facade. The self-proclaimed smartest one always forms the head.

entries.name: Amber Ambushers
entries: The hivebound champion's name comes from both the Oozes' golden colour and the scent they produce. The Oozes' bodies react with one another to produce a sickly sweet odour reminiscent of honey or mead, but the armour conceals the smell. The knight hunts by hiding a single, exposed Ooze in an open area while the rest of the body hides nearby. Once the scent of the exposed Ooze draws in prey, the rest are ready to clobber it into a soluble pile of meat.

entries.name: Playing the Fool
entries: Rats are a favorite prey for these knights, leading many to find homes in populated farm towns and cities. Once it discovers a few good hunting spots, a knight tends to stick around, using its armoured disguise to "blend in" with the local Humanoids. They rarely succeed in actually fooling anyone, but many homesteads and hamlets will play along with the charade, as a hivebound champion makes for excellent pest control.

entries.name: Fashion Statements
entries: Few knights have a full matching set of armour. Each champion will regularly trade pieces out, with different Oozes preferring different styles and alloys. With enough members, a knight may even end up with an extra arm, leg, or head. Though not ideal, a few "knights" have been seen wearing entire hollow logs until they can collect enough smaller pieces to build with.

monsterFluff.name: Primordial Wanderer
entries: {@i Insectoid Gatherers of Elemental Power}
{@b Habitat:} Planar (Elemental Planes); {@b Treasure:} Arcana
"{@i Haha! If it is the finest earth and water you seek, you've come to the right place! None but I carry the richest mud found deep beneath the waterways of the City of Spires!}"

Water, earth, fire, and air. As any Cosmology 101 attendee can attest, these four elements are crucial to understanding the fundamental principles of all matter—and to the primordial wanderers, no pursuit is nobler.

These insectoid Elementals make it their life's goal to uncover the mysteries lying in the borders of the Elemental Planes… and a few are even willing to share their findings with travelers—for a price.

entries.name: Planar Pilgrim
entries: To traverse the vast expanse of the Elemental Planes is no small feat, and each loop can take over eight years by the Material Plane's reckoning. The age of a primordial wanderer is measured by the number of journeys it has completed, regardless of how long each one took. Those who move too slowly, lingering in places for too long, inevitably fall behind their peers in age—a trait that is highly venerated by their society as a mark of wisdom and hard work. A wanderer with less than three or four pilgrimages is hardly worth listening to.

entries.name: On a Roll
entries: While the primary purpose behind each journey is to deepen their understanding of the world and chart the wonders of the elements, wanderers also spend time collecting samples of each element in its purest form. Each element is rolled together and compacted into a miniscule, portable bead of pure primordial power, which is highly sought after by prominent wizards and artificers. Even a single bead can go for a small fortune depending on its size, freshness, and purity.

entries.name: Homecoming
entries: A wanderer's pilgrimage starts and ends at the Nest, an ancient town nestled in the rocks of the Plane of Earth. Many take a few weeks between journeys to relax and celebrate their accomplishments, leaving the settlement a place of perpetual festivity. It's also an opportunity to trade the beads they've gathered for maps, tools, and other supplies for their next journey.
{@i Dodeshna, dodeshna! Awoo… ?}

entries.name: Fleadgling
entries: {@b Type:} Fey
{@b Creature Component:} Any Fey with a {@variantrule Fly Speed|XPHB}
{@b Bonus Tamer Improvement:} Growth I and +2 {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Dice}

Fleadglings are not the sharpest fleas in the caravan. Native to a peaceful region of the Fey Planes, these curious spirits are quick to leave their homes in search of adventure, eager to explore the wonders of the Material Plane; with a poor sense of direction and even poorer survival instincts, however, very few return to recount the tales of their travels.

Easily distracted, fleadglings follow whatever creature piques their interest at the moment. Tamers interested in training a fleadgling of their own are advised to decorate it with brightly coloured accessories in the very likely event it gets lost.
If any of the fleadgling's traits or actions require a saving throw, it is always against the fleadgling's save DC, as calculated below.

entries.name: Save DC
entries.text: 8 + the Tamer's Proficiency Bonus + the fleadgling's Strength modifier

entries.name: Monster Trainer
entries: If you are a Tamer, you can grant this familiar the following improvements using your Monster Trainer feature in addition to those normally available for your companions.

entries.name: Growth I
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Become a Tamer's companion. Type: Passive (Companion)}.

The fleadgling's size increases to Small, its {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Die} size increases to a d6 (its {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point maximum} consequently increases by 1 for each of its {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Dice}), and the damage die of its Headbutt attack increases to a d6.

entries.name: Charge!
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 3+ Tamer. Type: Passive (Companion)}.

If the fleadgling moves 15+ feet in a straight line toward a target immediately before hitting it with a melee attack roll, it can force the target to make a Strength saving throw. On a failed save, the target is knocked {@condition Prone|XPHB}.

entries.name: Invigorating Brew I
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 3+ Tamer. Type: Active (Bonus Action)}.

The fleadgling carries a bottle filled with a refreshing beverage. As a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB}, the fleadgling can drink from the bottle or administer it to an ally within 5 feet. A creature that drinks from the bottle gains the following benefits for 1 minute:

items.name: Improved Critical
entries: The creature's Critical Hit threshold is reduced by 1.

items.name: Speed
entries: The creature's {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} is increased by 15 feet.

items.name: Swiftness
entries: The creature doesn't provoke {@action Opportunity Attack|XPHB|Opportunity Attacks} when it moves out of an enemy's reach.
Once the fleadgling uses this {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB}, it can't do so again until its Tamer finishes a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short} or {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries.name: Growth II
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 5+ Tamer, Growth I. Type: Passive (Companion)}.

The fleadgling's size increases to Medium, its {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Die} size increases to a d8 (its {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point maximum} consequently increases by 1 for each of its {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Dice}), and the damage dice of its Headbutt attack increases to a d8.

entries.name: Multiattack
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 5+ Tamer. Type: Active (Action)}.
As an action, the fleadgling can make two attacks.

entries.name: Flea Bounce I
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 5+ Tamer. Type: Active (Action)}.

As an action, the fleadgling can fly a distance up to half its {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} toward a creature it can see (expending movement as normal), making one Headbutt attack against it if it's within reach. On a hit, the target takes an extra 14 ({@damage 4d6}) Bludgeoning damage, and the fledgling can bounce off the target towards another creature it can see, repeating the process. It can continue bouncing in this way until it misses an attack, makes three attacks, or runs out of movement.

The fleadgling can't attack the same creature more than once per use of this feature. Once the fleadgling uses this action, it can't use it again until its Tamer finishes a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short} or {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.
When the fleadgling's Tamer reaches level 9 in the Tamer class, the fleadgling can attack up to four targets before stopping.

entries.name: Invigorating Brew II
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 9+ Tamer, Invigorating Brew I. Type: Active (Bonus Action)}.
The benefits of the fleadgling's Invigorating Brew change to the following:

items.name: Improved Critical
entries: The creature's Critical Hit Threshold is reduced by 2.

items.name: Speed
entries: The creature's {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} is increased by 30 feet.

items.name: Swiftness
entries: The creature doesn't provoke {@action Opportunity Attack|XPHB|Opportunity Attacks} when it moves out of an enemy's reach, and it has {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} on Dexterity saving throws.

In addition, the fleadgling can use its Invigorating Brew I improvement twice, regaining all expended uses when its Tamer finishes a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short} or {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

When the fleadgling uses its {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} to offer its Invigorating Brew to an ally, the fleadgling can also gain the benefits of drinking from the bottle by expending two uses of this feature instead of one.

entries.name: Awoo!
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 9+ Tamer. Type: Active (Bonus Action)}.
As a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB}, the fleadgling can cast the {@spell Enthusing Cheer|HFN2025} spell, using Charisma as the spellcasting ability.
The fleadgling can cast this spell a number of times equal to its Charisma modifier (minimum of once), regaining all expended uses whenever its Tamer finishes a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries.name: Flea Bounce II
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 13+ Tamer, Flea Bounce I. Type: Active (Action)}.

The damage of the fleadgling's Flea Bounce I improvement increases to 17 ({@damage 5d6}). In addition, at the end of the turn that the fleadgling uses its Flea Bounce I improvement, the fleadgling gains {@variantrule Temporary Hit Points|XPHB} equal to 5 times the number of targets it hit with the action.
When the fleadgling's Tamer reaches level 17 in the Tamer class, the fleadgling can attack up to five targets before stopping.

entries.name: Growth III
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 13+ Tamer, Growth II. Type: Passive (Companion)}.

The fleadgling's size increases to Large, its {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Die} size increases to a d10 (its {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point maximum} consequently increases by 1 for each of its {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Dice}), and the damage dice of its Headbutt attack increases to a d10.

entries.name: Invigorating Brew III
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 17+ Tamer, Invigorating Brew II. Type: Active (Bonus Action)}.
A creature under the effects of the fleadgling's Invigorating Brew can make one additional attack when it takes the {@action Attack|XPHB} action on its turn.

entries.name: Mother Flea's Call
entries: {@i Prerequisite: Level 17+ Tamer. Type: Active (Action)}.

As a Magic action, the fleadgling can call upon the great Mother Flea, transforming into a {@creature Fluff of Fleas|HFN2025} for 1 minute or until it is reduced to 0 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} or recalled back into its vessel.

The fleadgling's game statistics are replaced by the stat block of the {@creature Fluff of Fleas|HFN2025}, but it retains its Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma scores. It also retains its saving throw proficiencies and uses its Tamer's Proficiency Bonus for them.

While transformed, the fleadgling assumes the {@creature Fluff of Fleas|HFN2025}' Hit Points and {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB|Hit Point Dice}. When the transformation ends, the fleadgling returns to the number of {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} it had before it transformed. If the transformation ends as a result of dropping to 0 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}, any excess damage carries over to the fleadgling's normal form.
Once the fleadgling uses this action, it can't use it again until its Tamer finishes a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

items.name: Bubble Barrier
entries: Three Medium oily bubbles sprout from the floor, wall, or ceiling in three different spaces of Architsune's choice that it can see within its lair. The bubbles remain until Initiative count 20 of the next round. When a creature other than Architsune comes within 5 feet of one of the bubbles, it explodes violently. The creature must make a {@dc 18} Constitution Saving Throw. On a failure, it takes 16 ({@damage 3d10}) Bludgeoning damage, and gains 1 level of Bubble Blight.

items.name: Greasy Shower
entries: A deluge of oil pours from the floor, wall, or ceiling. One creature Architsune can see in its lair must make a {@dc 18} Dexterity Saving Throw. On a failure, the target gains 1 level of Bubble Blight and is covered in slippery oil until Initiative count 20 of the next round. A creature covered in oil has {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Attack Rolls and Strength and Dexterity Ability Checks.

items.name: Slipping Hazard
entries: Oil covers the floor in a 20-foot Square that Architsune can see within its lair. This oil replicates the effects of the {@spell Grease|XPHB} spell (save {@dc 18}) and lasts until Initiative count 20 of the next round. A creature that fails its Saving Throw against this effect also gains 1 level of Bubble Blight.

items.name: Oleous Contamination
entries: All liquids within the lair, mundane or magical, become contaminated with oil. A creature that drinks any contaminated liquid must make a {@dc 18} Constitution Saving Throw. On a failure, it is {@condition Poisoned|XPHB} for 1 hour.

items.name: Surveillance Bubble
entries: While within its lair, Architsune can cast the {@spell Arcane Eye|XPHB} spell, requiring no components. When the spell is cast in this way, the eye takes the form of a large, shimmering bubble.

legendaryGroup.name: Zohtraferna
lairActions: The Zohtraferna's lair can't take the same action two turns in a row.

items.name: Prismatic Crystals
entries: A faint white glow fills the lair, and {@dice 1d4 + 2} crystals of pale magical energy appear in unoccupied spaces of the Zohtraferna's choice within its lair. Each crystal is 5 feet wide and 10 feet tall, has AC 16, and has 20 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}. If a crystal takes Fire, Force, Lightning, or Radiant damage, it explodes and is destroyed, dealing 9 ({@damage 2d8}) damage of the same type to each creature within 10 feet of it.

items.name: Brilliant Radiance
entries: Glowing lichens grow in three locations of the Zohtraferna's choice within its lair. Each lichen emits magical {@variantrule Bright Light|XPHB} in a 10-foot radius {@variantrule Sphere [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Sphere} centered on it. The light dispels {@spell Darkness|XPHB} created by 5th-level spells or lower. The light is grey, causing everything it touches to appear grey and colourless. The lichens and their light last until {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB} count 20 of the next round (winning ties).

items.name: Structural Collapse
entries: A large piece of crumbling ruin falls within the lair. One creature of the Zohtraferna's choice must make a {@dc 18} Dexterity Saving Throw. On a failure, the target takes 19 ({@damage 3d12}) Bludgeoning damage and is knocked {@condition Prone|XPHB}.

items.name: Evercast
entries: The sky within 1 mile of the lair is never dark, instead giving off a constant, grey {@variantrule Dim Light|XPHB}. Without other sources of light, everything in the area appears grey and colourless. Creatures other than Constructs do not typically inhabit the area within 6 miles of the lair.

items.name: Icetrap
entries: Milky, frost-like crystals cover every surface of the lair. A creature that finishes a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short} or {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB} within the lair is {@condition Restrained|XPHB} by the crystals. A creature can use an action to make a {@dc 15} Strength ({@skill Athletics|XPHB}) check, freeing itself or another creature within its reach on a success.

items.name: Infectious Rage
entries: The infectious rage carried by the Morphelean tears through the local wildlife wherever it wanders. Beasts within 5 miles of the Morphelean are Hostile to the party and attack if approached. The Morphelean can be tracked through the blood and corpses left in its wake with a successful {@dc 10} Wisdom ({@skill Survival|XPHB}) check.

items.name: Perpetual Dark
entries: Day and night are warped by the Morphelean's curse. The area within 1 mile of the Morphelean is blanketed in perpetual {@spell Darkness|XPHB}. This Darkness dissipates if the Morphelean enters its Imago Form.

legendaryGroup.name: Chimerina
lairActions: The scent of rotting meat means one of two things: something big didn't finish its dinner, or the scent is false and you have wandered into a chimerina's lair. Beetles and other detritivores are abundant, drawn by the lure of a potential feast. For those without the ability to smell, a Rotscent Lair appears unremarkable, a forest or jungle like any other.
On its turn, the Rotscent Lair takes an action to cause one of the following effects. The lair can't take the same action two turns in a row.

items.name: Alluring Aroma
entries: An alluring smell fills the air around a point in the lair. Each creature in a 60-foot-radius {@variantrule Sphere [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Sphere} centered on that point must make a {@dc 14} Wisdom Saving Throw. On a failure, at the start of the target's next turn, it immediately moves up to its {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} toward that point by the shortest route that avoids obviously damaging terrain (GM's discretion).

items.name: Paralysing Thread
entries: A thin, almost-invisible thread falls from the sky, partially paralysing creatures it touches. Each creature in a 60-foot-long, 5-foot-wide {@variantrule Line [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Line} originating from a point in the lair must make a {@dc 14} Constitution Saving Throw. On a failure, the target rolls {@dice 1d6} and suffers the following effect until {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB} count 20 of the following round (winning ties):

items.name: 1-2: Arm
entries: The creature has {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Attack Rolls.

items.name: 3-4: Leg
entries: The creature's {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} is halved, and it has {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Strength and Dexterity checks and {@variantrule Saving Throw|XPHB|Saving Throws}.

items.name: 5-6: Head
entries: The creature can't talk or cast spells with Verbal components, and Attack Rolls against it have {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB}.

items.name: Summon Swarm
entries: A {@creature Swarm of Insects|XMM} (lair's choice of beetles, centipedes, or wasps) appears in the lair and is Hostile to creatures to whom the Chimerina is Hostile.

items.name: Miasma
entries: The awful scent of rotting meat pervades the area within 1 mile of the lair, robbing creatures of their appetite. Each creature that takes a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short} or {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB} in the area must make a {@dc 14} Constitution Saving Throw. On a failure, the target can't eat during the rest, and it regains only half as many {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} from {@variantrule Hit Dice|XPHB} it spends at the end of a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short}, or half its {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point} total at the end of a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. In addition, for a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}, its {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} level doesn't decrease.

legendaryGroup.name: Painted Lady
lairActions: On its turn, the Chroma Cavern takes an action to cause the following effect.

items.name: Swirling Canvas
entries: The living paint within the lair changes the entire area into a beautiful landscape painting of the lady's choice from the following options. Objects, textures, and depth within the artwork are physical, and the effects last until the landscape disappears or changes. The lair can't use the same option two rounds in a row.

items.name: Frozen Sea
entries: The ground in the lair freezes over in slick ice and becomes {@variantrule Difficult Terrain|XPHB}. Each creature on the ground must make a {@dc 19} Dexterity Saving Throw. On a failure, the target falls {@condition Prone|XPHB}.

items.name: Snowy Woodland
entries: Blowing snow fills the lair. Ranged Attacks have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB}.
